lol-meta-tracker · draft model, v0 → v0.8.1

How the draft model actually works

Four ideas, in the order they happen: prediction is a ranking contest, a tree learns thresholds by trying all of them, boosting is a relay of error-fixers, and the scoreboard grades every strategy against what the pros really picked.

Step 1 · The game

Prediction here means ranking, not guessing

Freeze one real moment: EWC semifinal, Karmine Corp on blue side, second pick. Some champions are banned or taken; about 140 are still legal. Each legal champion is a candidate, described by a few numbers — how meta it is lately, how often this team plays it.

The model gives every candidate a score: "how likely is this the one that gets locked in?" Then we sort. The prediction is just the top of the sorted list — the same way a search engine scores every document against your query and shows the best ones first.

One decision, eight of the ~140 candidates

KC, blue side, pick 2. Press the button to score & sort. The gold row is the ground truth — what KC actually picked.

Unsorted: this is what the moment looks like before any strategy weighs in.

Top-k accuracy is just: after sorting, did the real pick land in the top 1? Top 3? Top 5? That's the whole grading system — for the model and for every baseline.

Step 2 · One tree

A tree learns a threshold by trying every one

A decision tree is a stack of if-else questions like pick_rate > 0.17? Nobody hand-writes the 0.17. The algorithm tries every possible cut, and for each one asks: how cleanly did this separate the picked rows from the not-picked rows?

Drag the cut yourself and watch the two buckets. Then let the algorithm do what it does: sweep every position and keep the winner.

16 candidate rows on one feature

Each dot is one candidate row from the training data, placed by its pick_rate. Gold dots were actually picked (label = 1); grey were not (label = 0).

Left bucket · below the cut
Right bucket · above the cut
Separation quality:

That's one question learned. The tree then repeats the same search inside each bucket — maybe splitting the right bucket on team_usage, the left on is_ban — up to six questions deep in our model. Which feature to split on isn't chosen in advance either: at every step the algorithm auditions all eleven features and keeps whichever split separates best.

The "separation quality" number here is a simplified version of what sklearn computes (it's called impurity reduction, or Gini gain). Same idea: reward cuts that make each bucket more single-colored than the whole was.

Step 3 · Many trees

Boosting is a relay, not a vote on overlap

One correction to your mental model: we don't grow lots of trees and then look for where they overlap or agree. That's a different technique (random forests are closer to that). Boosting builds trees one at a time, in sequence — and each new tree is trained specifically on the errors the previous trees left behind.

Tree 1 learns the obvious rule ("meta champions get picked"). Wherever it's still wrong, tree 2 focuses. Wherever they're still wrong, tree 3 focuses. The final score is the running sum of all corrections — our run stopped at 345 trees.

Watch the relay fix errors

Eight candidate rows. Bars are the model's current score; the gold tick is the truth it's aiming for (1 = was picked, 0 = wasn't).

Total error:
Step 4 · The scoreboard

What "beating the baseline" means

Here's the confusion to clear up: the baseline is not what actually happened. What actually happened — the pro's real pick — is the answer key. A baseline is a competing strategy: a deliberately simple rule that also produces a ranked list, so we can ask whether the model earns its complexity.

We ran three strategies over the same 860 blind decisions from the EWC main event. Each one sorts the same candidates; each is graded by where the answer key lands in its list:

Same decision, same truth, three orderings

One illustrative decision. The gold row is the actual pick — watch where each strategy ranks it.

Model (345 trees)
  1. 1Vi
  2. 2Orianna ←
  3. 3Rumble
  4. 4Jayce
  5. 5Syndra
truth at #2 → top-3 hit
Meta baseline
  1. 1Vi
  2. 2Jayce
  3. 3Syndra
  4. 4Orianna ←
  5. 5Azir
truth at #4 → top-5 hit only
Team-habit baseline
  1. 1Rumble
  2. 2Gnar
  3. 3Xin Zhao
  4. 4Camille
  5. 5Annie
truth at #9 → miss

The original v0 blind test — 860 decisions (kept for the record)

Trained only on games before July 15. Hover any bar for detail. Dashed line = random guessing among ~140 legal champions. This was v0 on the pre-finals test set — Step 5 regrades everything, v0.8 included, on the full 1,000 decisions.

Model Meta baseline — rank by 28-day pick/ban rate Team habit — rank by this team's own usage
Top-1
Top-3
Top-5
What we beat

Everything, at top-1. Calling the exact next pro action from ~140 legal options 10.7% of the time is 15× random — and picks improve over the meta baseline at every k.

What we didn't

Bans at top-3/top-5. Pros mostly ban the same few meta terrors, so "rank by presence" nearly solves bans — the model's extra features add noise there, not signal.

Why the gap is small — and useful

v0 sees each candidate's rates, but not which champions are already on the board. Synergy, counters, "they have no AD" — all invisible to it. That measured gap is exactly the case for v1: champion embeddings and sequence context.

Step 5 · The scoreboard, updated for v0.8

The test set grew up — and so did the model

When this page first went up, every number below came from 860 blind decisions across 43 EWC games — the main event minus its finals, because the finals hadn't been played yet. That test set has since grown to its full, final size: 1,000 decisions across all 50 games of the EWC July 2026 main event, finals included. The 10.7% top-1 figure quoted earlier in this piece is the old v0 model on the old 860. Everything below is graded fresh, on the full 1,000, so v0 and v0.8 are being compared on ground that didn't exist when v0 shipped.

Two things changed in the model itself between then and now. v0.7 is the same gradient-boosted-tree idea from Steps 2–3, grown into a 10-model ensemble and refit on multi-year data (2024–2026) instead of 2026 alone. v0.8 is new: a small causal transformer that learns its own champion embeddings and reads the actual sequence of picks and bans on the board — the upgrade Step 7 promised. v0.8 shipped as a blend of the two, weighted 0.25 toward the transformer; v0.8.1, the current cut, splits that into separate weights per decision type — picks lean 0.75 transformer, bans use the GBM alone — chosen on validation only.

Six strategies, the same 1,000 decisions

EWC July 2026 main event, all 50 games including the finals. Same grading rule as Step 4 above: did the real pick or ban land in the top 1 / top 3 / top 5 of each strategy's ranked list?

All decisions Picks only Bans only
Strategy Top-1Top-3Top-5 Top-1Top-3Top-5 Top-1Top-3Top-5
Meta baseline 9.9%26.6%38.0% 7.4%21.6%32.8% 12.4%31.6%43.2%
Team-habit baseline 5.9%13.1%20.1% 6.0%12.4%19.8% 5.8%13.8%20.4%
v0.7 GBM ensemble (multi-year refit) 14.9%32.8%45.2% 14.8%32.2%45.4% 15.0%33.4%45.0%
v0.8 transformer (solo) 10.2%22.7%30.9% 12.8%28.6%37.4% 7.6%16.8%24.4%
v0.8 blend (w=0.25) 14.1%32.6%44.5% 17.2%35.2%48.0% 11.0%30.0%41.0%
v0.8.1 per-type blend (picks w=0.75, bans w=0) 16.0%33.1%44.6% 17.0%32.8%44.2% 15.0%33.4%45.0%

n = 1,000 decisions total (500 picks, 500 bans). "Big" figures mark each model's top-1 columns for quick scanning — that's the number that matters most: did the strategy call the exact next action.

Here's the honest verdict, no hedging: the transformer does not beat the GBM ensemble. On its own, across all 1,000 decisions, it gets 10.2% top-1 against the ensemble's 14.9% — a clear loss, not a rounding error. If v0.8 shipped as "replace the trees with a sequence model," that would be a regression.

But v0.8 didn't ship that way — it shipped as a blend, and the blend tells a different story. Blended at a 0.25 weight, it ties the GBM ensemble overall (14.1% vs. 14.9% top-1 on all decisions) and clearly wins on picks (17.2% vs. 14.8% top-1) — a real gain, not noise, on exactly the half of the problem Step 7 said the old model was blind to. The other half of the story is the mirror image: on bans alone, the transformer's top-1 is 7.6% against the GBM's 15.0% — barely half. That's not a modeling weakness so much as a design consequence: the transformer is date-blind. It never sees what month or patch a game happened in, so it has no way to track which champions the current meta is banning into oblivion. The GBM's rate-based features carry that signal directly; the transformer has to infer it from board state alone, and can't.

So the real finding is a division of labor: the transformer owns picks, the GBM owns bans, and the blend's job is just knowing which to trust for which decision. There's also a smaller, separate win worth naming — it has nothing to do with the transformer. Refitting the same v0.7 feature set on 2024–2026 data instead of 2026-only lifted top-1 accuracy from 13.0% to 14.9%, a +1.9 point gain from more training years alone, before the transformer enters the picture at all.

v0.8.1 acts on that division of labor directly: instead of one blend weight for everything, it selects a separate weight per decision type on validation data — picks at 0.75 transformer, bans at 0, meaning bans just use the GBM. Result: 16.0% top-1 overall, the best of any lineage here, with bans back at the GBM's full 15.0%. Two honesty flags, stated plainly. First, this was our second evaluation against the same EWC test set (v0.8 was the first), so treat the margin with that caveat — we froze it and will not tune against this test set again. Second, the transformer-heavy pick weight looked clearly better on validation (19.1% top-1) but did not transfer: on test, picks came out at 17.0%, a hair below the flat blend's 17.2% and behind it at top-3/5. The overall gain is entirely the bans coming home.

What we beat

Both baselines, at every k, by every strategy that isn't the transformer solo. And on picks specifically, the blend's 17.2% top-1 beats not just the meta baseline (7.4%) but the GBM ensemble's own picks number (14.8%) — the clearest single win in this round.

What we didn't

The transformer, unblended, still can't touch the GBM anywhere — and loses hardest on bans (7.6% vs. 15.0% top-1) because it can't see what patch it's on. Embeddings bought board-awareness, not meta-awareness.

What it means

Rung 3 wasn't a wrong bet — it was an incomplete one. A model that reads the board wins at picks, where synergy and counters live. Bans are a different problem: they're about tracking what's hot right now, which is exactly the signal the transformer was built to ignore.

Step 6 · Predictions in public

We also made predictions in public — here's how they scored

The day before EWC finals, the project published a data-driven preview — series odds, expected bans, a draft read — with explicit predictions, not hedges. After the games finished, every call was scored against what actually happened, verified against Oracle's Elixir, gol.gg, and Liquipedia.

Series calls

Both winners were called at roughly 55/45. Both landed — but not the same way.

SeriesPredictedActualVerdict
3rd placeGen.G 2-1 (~55/45)Gen.G 2-1Exact — winner and scoreline
Grand FinalDplus Kia 3-2 (~55/45)Dplus Kia 3-0Right winner, wrong shape

The Bo5 read badly overrated how competitive it would be: DK's three wins ran 31:20, 33:40, 27:09 with a combined 57-22 kill score. A five-game war was called; a sweep is what showed up.

Draft reads: roughly 5-for-9

The preview also called specific champions — who'd be banned, who'd be feared, who'd get picked. Some of that mapped the games exactly. Some of it didn't.

Hits
Vi/Jayce/Orianna ban triangle — called as the decisive contested pool; Vi, Jayce, and Poppy were banned in all six games of finals day.

The Nocturne paradox — flagged as unpicked-all-day dead weight; KC burned a ban on it anyway in game 1 of the final, the exact "wasted ban" scenario called in advance, in a game KC lost.

Camille support as live tech — showed up in game 1 of both series, at support, exactly as flagged.

Viktor as DK's scariest release — ShowMaker first-picked it in game 1 of the final and won with it.
Misses
Rumble respect — predicted DK would open with a Rumble ban every game. DK never banned it once; they dared KC to pick it. Canna did in game 3 and lost 23-5.

Lucian+Milio as "the premier bot duo" — Milio appeared zero times across six games, no pick, no ban.

KC's expected ban targets (Lee Sin, Taliyah, Viktor) — none of the three was ever banned by KC. KC picked Lee Sin themselves in game 1.

Team-level form stats called both winners, one exact scoreline. Champion-level reads went roughly 5-for-9 — excellent on which champions would define the ban phase, wrong wherever a read required a team to fear what the market feared. Presence stats say what's contested; they don't say which team blinks.

Step 7 · Under the hood

Why a laptop CPU is enough — and why no LLM

The library we used is scikit-learn's HistGradientBoostingClassifier. The "Hist" is the whole secret. It felt trivial because two decades of algorithm engineering are hiding inside one .fit() call.

The histogram trick. Step 2 said the algorithm "tries every split." Taken literally, that's brutal: 4 million rows × 11 features, sorted and scanned at every node of every tree. Instead, the library first compresses each feature into at most 256 buckets — every pick_rate value gets snapped to one of 256 bins, once, up front. Now "try every split" means scanning 256 bucket boundaries instead of 4 million raw values:

4,000,000 values → 256 buckets → scan the boundaries

A feature's histogram. Finding the best split = walking the bucket edges once and scoring each — the orange bin marks the winner.

pick_rate binned into buckets; each bar = how many training rows landed there

Do the arithmetic and the mystery evaporates. One node: 11 features × 256 bins ≈ 3,000 candidate splits. One tree of depth 6: ~63 nodes ≈ 190k split evaluations, each a handful of additions. All 345 trees: on the order of tens of billions of simple arithmetic operations — and a modern laptop core does billions of those per second, across several cores. A minute of CPU is genuinely all this costs. GPUs earn their keep when models have millions of learned weights multiplying huge matrices; a forest of if-else questions has no matrices to multiply.

Why no LLM? An LLM is a model of language — its job is unstructured text, and it needs billions of weights because language is that messy. Our data is already structured: rows, columns, numbers, labels. For that shape of problem, boosted trees have been the reigning champion for ~20 years — they win most tabular Kaggle competitions to this day, against neural networks too. The lesson worth keeping: match the tool to the structure of the data. LLMs enter this project later, if ever — maybe explaining drafts in prose, never for scoring 140 candidates.

Why it felt trivial: the algorithm is a solved problem you import. What was ours — deciding a decision is a ranking contest, keeping features strictly point-in-time, masking fearless champions, splitting by time, choosing honest baselines — is the actual ML engineering. The library has no opinion on any of that, and every way v0 could have silently lied lives there, not in .fit().

Step 8 · The code

The five moving pieces, in the real code

Everything lives in two scripts in the repo: scripts/draft_dataset.py (turn games into decisions) and scripts/train_draft_model.py (train and grade). Trimmed to their load-bearing lines:

① Replay the draft, one decision at a time

# the standard draft sequence: (order, type, side, team-ordinal)
DRAFT_SEQUENCE = [
    (1, "ban", "Blue", 1), (2, "ban", "Red", 1), ...
    (7, "pick", "Blue", 1), (8, "pick", "Red", 1), ...
]

taken = set(unavailable_series)  # fearless: champs burned earlier in the series
for seq, dtype, side, ordinal in DRAFT_SEQUENCE:
    actual = rec[side]["bans" if dtype == "ban" else "picks"][ordinal - 1]
    avail = [c for c in champs if c not in taken]  # the candidate list
    ...emit one row per available candidate, label = (c == actual)...
    taken.add(actual)  # the board state advances

This loop is Step 1. Each pass emits one mini ranking contest; taken grows as the draft unfolds, so later decisions have fewer legal candidates — and in fearless leagues the set starts pre-loaded with the series' used champions.

② Features that only know the past

def _window(self, cum, day, days):
    # sum of daily counts over [day - days, day - 1] —
    # strictly BEFORE the game being predicted
    hi = self.didx[day] - 1
    lo = hi - days
    return cum[hi] - cum[lo]

pick_rate = games_picked_last_28d / games_played_last_28d
team_usage = this_team_games_with_champ_56d / this_team_games_56d

The -1 is the leakage discipline in one character: rates are computed through yesterday. Include the game itself and the model "predicts" a pick it was already shown — the classic way sports models cheat by accident.

③ Split by time, never at random

is_test = (ds.league == "EWC") & (ds.date.dt.month == 7)
cutoff = ds.loc[is_test, "date"].min()   # first EWC main-event game
train = ds[~is_test & (ds.date < cutoff)]  # 1,447 games, all before it
test  = ds[is_test]                        # 43 blind games

A random split would scatter EWC games into training — the model would be quizzed on a meta it had already seen. Time-ordered splits are what makes "blind test" an honest phrase.

④ Training is one call — the library does Steps 2 and 3

model = HistGradientBoostingClassifier(
    max_iter=600,        # up to 600 relay runners (trees)
    learning_rate=0.08,  # each tree corrects gently, not all at once
    max_depth=6,         # six if-else questions per tree
    early_stopping=True, # quit when held-out error stops improving
)
model.fit(train[FEATURES], train["label"])   # → stopped itself at 345 trees

Every threshold search from Step 2 and the whole error-fixing relay from Step 3 happen inside this one line — histogram-binned, multi-core, about a minute.

⑤ Grading: sort each contest, find the truth

def hit_rank(g):
    # one decision: sort its candidates by score, descending
    order = np.argsort(-g["model_score"])
    return np.argmax(g["label"][order])  # where did truth land?

top1 = (ranks < 1).mean()   # fraction of decisions where truth was #1
top3 = (ranks < 3).mean()
top5 = (ranks < 5).mean()

Step 4's entire scoreboard is these five lines run three times — once with the model's scores, once ranking by meta rates, once by team habit. Same contests, same answer key, three orderings.

Step 9 · What's next

Roles, flex picks, and what "embeddings" actually means

v0's blind spot: it knows every candidate's rates but not which champions are already on the board. Two upgrades attack that, and the first one came from watching drafts, not from a textbook: champions live in positional pools, and flex picks hide information on purpose.

The data can see it. Player rows record position, so we can measure exactly how role-committed every champion is. Of 96 champions with 15+ games in 2026, 16 are true flex picks (two or more roles at 15%+ share):

The real flex pool, 2026 pro play

Share of each champion's games by position. Everyone else is 85%+ locked to one role.

ChampionTopJngMidBotSupGames
Ambessa83%17%–––296
Aurora20%–80%––282
Varus18%––82%–236
Anivia19%–67%–13%181
Poppy13%55%––32%93
Ziggs––21%79%–91
Mel––58%41%–86
Shen56%10%––34%68

Your intuition, confirmed by the data: Anivia really is a mid/top/support flex, Poppy really is jungle/support/top. And your constraint logic — "Poppy could still be jungle unless a support is already locked" — is exactly how the feature should work: a pick doesn't close a role, it shifts the probabilities over which roles remain open, and each new pick propagates the constraints.

Concretely, v1 can carry features like "probability this candidate fills a role the team still needs, given the likeliest role assignment of its picks so far." That's computable from the flex table above — no neural anything required — and it directly encodes the mind-game you described with Viktor and Poppy.

And embeddings? Today, "Orianna" enters the model only as a bundle of rates — the model cannot know that Orianna-the-champion is on the board, or that she's similar to Azir and synergizes with dive junglers. An embedding fixes that: give every champion a learned list of ~32 numbers — coordinates in a space where champions that play similar draft roles end up near each other. Nobody writes the coordinates; they're trained, the same way the trees found their own thresholds: start random, nudge them whenever they mispredict a draft, and structure emerges — flex picks drift together, engage supports cluster, "counters X" becomes a direction. Then a sequence model reads the actual champions picked so far, as coordinates, and predicts the next one. That's rung 3: it's how the model finally gets to see the board.

What the draft model learned about champions, unsupervised

t-SNE of the v0.8 embedding space, colored by primary role. Ringed points are flex picks -- the model places them between role clusters on its own.

5-NN role purity in the raw embedding space: 0.682, against a 0.20 chance floor for five roles -- and the model was never shown a single role label. It organized champions this cleanly by role purely from co-occurrence patterns in draft sequences.

Step 9 · revisited

The rung-3 promise, kept — half of it

The first version of this page ended this very step with a promise: give every champion a learned list of ~32 numbers, let a sequence model read the actual champions picked so far as coordinates, and predict the next one from that. "That's rung 3: it's how the model finally gets to see the board."

That model got built. It's a small causal transformer — four layers, 192-dimensional embeddings, six attention heads — trained on 100,836 decisions across three years of drafts, learning its own champion coordinates from scratch with no injected domain knowledge about synergy or counters. And it works, exactly where the promise said it would: picks, where "they have no engage" and "this comps into dive" are draft-reading judgments a rate table can't make. That's the transformer's entire margin over the GBM on picks.

What the promise left out: seeing the board turned out to cost the model its sense of when it is. A sequence of champions carries no timestamp, so the transformer that reads it perfectly still can't tell a patch-16.13 ban meta from a patch-16.9 one — and bans are almost entirely about the current meta. Rung 3 got built, and it does what it was built to do. It just wasn't built to do everything, and the honest result says so plainly.

lol-meta-tracker · first written for v0, updated after v0.8 · trained on 2024–2026 pro drafts (100,836 training decisions) · blind-tested on the EWC July 2026 main event incl. finals (50 games / 1,000 decisions) · scripts/draft_dataset.py + scripts/train_draft_model_v08.py
